coredata.dc:title: Non-precious metal-based integrated electrodes for overall alkaline water splitting
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Indian Chemical Society
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Indian Chemical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Indian Chemical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Manojkumar, Kaliyannan
Kandeeban, Rajagopalan
Brindha, Ramasubramanian
Sangeetha, Velusamy
Saminathan, Kulandaivel
coredata.dc:description: A bifunctional electrocatalyst interface requires a superior charge transfer and good electric conductivity to produce an efficient and stable water splitting reaction. In the context of controlling the electrochemical activity of bifunctional catalytic materials, we demonstrate a novel approach to bridge conductive C-Qd-Mn interfaces with precise control. The excellent performance for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) can be dominantly attributed to the unique structure of C-Qds-Mn, which provides abundant active sites and facilitates electron transfer between electrode and electrolyte. Eco-friendly C-Qds were synthesized using a large-scale thermal assisted technique and utilized as electrocatalysts with Mn3O4 in alkaline splitting ToF (Turn over Frequency) calculation to make an effective combination. Due to their increased ECSA, conductivity, and electron-hole transfer, the prepared C-Qds-Mn delivered excellent HER/OER (0.494 V/1.6671V) activity, lower Standard deviation 0.006 V and 0.009V, surpassing the 10 mA limit comparable to commercially.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Mn3O4
Alkaline splitting
OER
HER

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the C-Qds-Mn used in alkaline splitting.
a) CQDs- XRD spectrum b) CQDs -Raman spectrum c) C-Qds –HR TEM d) CQDs- XPS analysis e) XPS Carbon bond analysis of CQDs f) XRD Spectrum of Mn3O4.
a) Cyclic voltammogram of electrodes a) C-Qds-Mn, b) C-Qds c) Mn3O4 at different scan rates. d) Plot shows the linear regression fit lines between scan rate and ΔJ/2 at 1.2 V to obtain Cdl.

a) LSV curves towards HER for prepared electrodes b) Tafel curves for OER c) Stability test of C-Qds-Mn towards HER d) LSV curves towards OER for different electrodes e) Tafel curves of the materials and composite for OER in 6 M KOH with scan rate of 5 m.

head.ce:title: Non-precious metal-based integrated electrodes for overall alkaline water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Kongunadu arts and Science College, Coimbatore, 641029, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Kongunadu arts and Science College. Coimbatore, India - 641029

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National University of Singapore, 119077, Singapore
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National University of Singapore, Singapore - 119077

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A bifunctional electrocatalyst interface requires a superior charge transfer and good electric conductivity to produce an efficient and stable water splitting reaction. In the context of controlling the electrochemical activity of bifunctional catalytic materials, we demonstrate a novel approach to bridge conductive C-Qd-Mn interfaces with precise control. The excellent performance for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) can be dominantly attributed to the unique structure of C-Qds-Mn, which provides abundant active sites and facilitates electron transfer between electrode and electrolyte. Eco-friendly C-Qds were synthesized using a large-scale thermal assisted technique and utilized as electrocatalysts with Mn3O4 in alkaline splitting ToF (Turn over Frequency) calculation to make an effective combination. Due to their increased ECSA, conductivity, and electron-hole transfer, the prepared C-Qds-Mn delivered excellent HER/OER (0.494 V/1.6671V) activity, lower Standard deviation 0.006 V and 0.009V, surpassing the 10 mA limit comparable to commercially.

Schematic illustration of the C-Qds-Mn used in alkaline splitting.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline water splitting (AWS) is one of the sustainable methods of producing hydrogen gas [1,2]. AWS is less expensive than proton exchange membrane water splitting because of non-platinum metal catalysts [3]. Further, AWS is significantly influenced by strategically combining active catalysts and composite structures for generating distinct elementary chemical pathways with improved performance [4]. Carbon compounds are valuable materials for AWS because of their high conductivity, structural flexibility, and ubiquity [5,6]. Carbon has already received much attention in electrochemistry, photoelectron devices, and electrocatalysts. Recently, C-Qds attracted the attention of scientists due to their low toxicity, water solubility, excellent conductivity, and high chemical stability [7]. However, electrocatalyst application of C-Qds requires two essential ideas for improving its eco-friendliness and efficiency, (i) Usage of sustainable techniques for the synthesis of C-Qds (ii) composite optimization of C-Qds/metal oxide.

The evolution of hydrogen by electrolysis in alkaline water is a crucial energy conversion process, especially for some discontinuous energy systems. However, its practical application is limited by low energy conversion efficiency and the need for expensive electrocatalysts. In response, carbon-based quantum dots received considerable interest as promising replacements for the precious metal catalysts currently in use. Among them, carbon dots (CQDs) exhibit many outstanding characteristics, with flexible composition, strong metal coordination good dispersion, and. high electrical conductivity. Previous decade has seen significant progress in carbon quantum dot based electrocatalysts for HER, where CQDs and their derivative non precious metal based electrocatalyst have shown remarkable performance and much-needed prospects, essential for the future use of hydrogen production.

However, the widespread use of CQDs-based electrocatalysts still requires the development of high-quality DCs and advanced synthesis strategies. New evaluation techniques are needed to understand the unique functions of DC in composition regulation, structure formation, surface modification, and interaction with hosts in the catalyst and finally, the relationship between structure and composition is established.

Hydrogen is the cleanest source of energy in the energy economy. Electrocatalytic AWS is considered an efficient and environmentally friendly process for sustainable hydrogen production. To date, platinum (Pt) has been the element of choice to catalyze hydrogen evolution (HER) reactions due to its high current density and low voltage rise. However, the high cost and limited Pt reserves hindered the technological scalability of this alternative.

Carbon based nanomaterials have been widely applied in the fabrication of electrocatalysts.With excellent tunable surface physicochemical properties, thermal stability and corrosion resistance to acids and alkalis, The low-cost Manganese Oxide with Mixed Valence (Mn3O4) has received increasing attention due to its relative high activity and superior stability; loading Mn3O4 Nanoparticles on Conductive Supports can also enhance its Catalytic Performance owing to the Enhancement of Electrical Conductivity. Carbon Based Quantum Dots (CBDs) Catalysts with Smaller Size Show Superior Electrocatalytic Performance Because the Quantum Confinement and Edge Effects Are Particularly Advantageous. A Conductive Carbon Support Can Ameliorate the Low Electrical Conductivity of the Mn3O4Catalysts

Efforts to improve the electrocatalytic activity of alkaline splitting processes have previously been made. For effective OER and HER activities, Sn, Ni, B, Co, Fe, P doped catalysts were used recently [8–14]. These materials, on the contrary, are costly, polluting, and not developed in an eco-friendly pathway. As the globe faces more climate issues, it's critical to find ways to reuse or recycle materials [15–19], leading to a circular economy and sustainable progress in hydrogen generation [20,21]. In this study, C-Qds were synthesized from solid waste using a novel large-scale then all aided recycling technique, then combined with Mn3O4 nanospheres and employed as an electrocatalyst for OER and HER activity in an alkaline electrolyte medium (Fig. 1 ).

This work is low-toxic, cost-effective and for the first time, we reported large-scale recycled material as electrocatalyst in alkaline splitting. The effectiveness of the manganese oxide (Mn3O4) catalyst was improved by mixing with C-Qds. Pristine Mn3O4 catalysts have low HER activity, but when combined with C-Qds, the total electrochemical surface area (ECSA) and conductivity of the catalyst increases, resulting in more active sites for OER/HER. The C-Qds-Mn catalyst has the potential to accelerate future H2 fuel generation. C-Qds-Mn delivered excellent HER/OER (0.494 V/1.6671V) activity, surpassing the 10 mA limit comparable to commercially available benchmark catalysts.

Manganese acetate, ethylene glycol, potassium permanganate (KMnO4) Carbon sheet (1 × 6 cm), Platinum wire (1mm × 6 cm), potassium hydroxide (KOH), ethanol, DMSO (Dimethyl sulphoxide) were purchased from Merck chemicals, USA. All the chemicals were analytical grade and are used without further purification. Chopped paper compost was dried under sunlight for 15 days in the ambient environment. Then the compost was hewn in the chopper followed by sintering at 700 °C under the inert atmosphere to form carbonaceous material. A dried foil capped 250 mL conical flask equipped with a magnetic pellet was filled with 2 g of carbon material, 100 mL of DMSO and stirred at room temperature. After 12 h of stirring, the resultant mixture was sonicated for 30 min. Then the mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm to eliminate sediments. The supernatant liquid was filtered with a 0.22-μm filter to remove bigger particles leaving the concentrated material, which was washed with ethanol and used for further analysis. To synthesis Mn3O4, IFB 20pm1s with 1200 W power microwave was used. Manganese oxide was synthesized using microwave oven for faster reaction rate with less time consumption. Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) dissolved in polyethylene glycol was added dropwise with an interval of 5 min to 3 ml of C4H6MnO4 solution. At each interval, the solution was treated in a microwave oven for 1 min. This allows for the forming of nanoscale particles rather than macro structures. The solution with precipitated Mn3O4 was washed with water and dried in microwave.

C-Qds, Mn3O4 were mixed with PVDF followed by the addition of a few drops of N-Methyl pyrodiline, and mechanically stirred to make slurry. The optimized composition paste (78% C-Qds + 20% Mn3O4 + 2% PVDF) was dried on carbon paper, heated in oven at 500 °C for 6 h. Then the collected samples (C-Qds -Mn) were used for analysis [22].

The surface functional groups were analysed using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Shimadzu, Japan) in the spectral range of 400–4000 cm−1 at room temperature. Raman spectrum was recorded using Horiba-Jobin, HR spectrometer with argon laser sources of 514.5 nm. The UV spectrum was recorded using JASCO UV Vis NIR-670 spectroscopy to examine the absorbance range of the C-Qds. Structural properties of the prepared C-Qds were studied using X-ray diffraction (Shimadzu XRD -6000, Japan) with Cukα radiation source. High Resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, JEM-2100 HR TEM) was used to study the morphological characteristics of the prepared C-Qds. Photoluminescence spectrometer (FLS1000) was used to analyze the multiphoton activation of C-Qds with luminescence property.

To evaluate the electrochemical surface area (ECSA), a three-electrode setup was used. Carbon sheet with C-Qds was used as the working electrodes while calomel (SCE) and platinum mesh were used as reference and counter electrode respectively. The density of the deposited catalysts was maintained at 0.2 mg/cm2. The cyclic voltammogram was conducted using 6 M KOH electrolyte solution at a scan rate of 0.05 V/s. Linear sweep voltammogram (LSV) was performed with rotating disk electrode (RDE) to obtain the polarization curves for OER and HER in O2 saturated 6 M KOH at 0.005 V/s scan rate and 1600 rpm rotating speed. The durability was evaluated after 1500 cycles in LSV.

The X-ray diffraction patterns show peaks at 2θ = 10.4°, 19.6°, and 22.2°, 38.9°, and 43° confirming the presence of C-Qds in agreement with JCPDS (82–0505), and the corresponding (hkl) planes were (200), (311), (533), and (711). The peaks at 22.2° and 43° confirm the presence of graphene characteristics with FCC (Face centred cubic) structure [23] (Fig. 2 a). Raman spectrum shows the D and G band at 1350 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 respectively. Sharp peaks of the D and G band; indicate that the C-Qds remain in the solution state. The 2D band was observed at 2854 cm−1 confers the multi phonon process. The intensity ratio between ID: IG was 0.71 [23,24], which shows that the synthesized C-Qds have graphene nature with low impurities (Fig. 2b). The C-Qds structural morphology was analysed by transmission electron microscopy, which revealed that particle sizes ranged from 3.0 to 6.0 nm, with an average size of 5.1 nm (Fig. 2c) [25]. The XPS method was used to determine the surface composition and elemental analysis of the carbon dots generated. These carbon dots are constituted of atomic C, H and O, according to the XPS data. Carbon dots have three contributions to the C 1 s spectrum: 283.4 eV, 284.0 eV and 286.0eV. The produced carbon structure may be attributed to the initial and primary contribution at 284.0 eV. 286.0eV represents carbon oxygen distribution (Fig. 2d and e) [26]. The well-indexed diffraction peaks of Mn3O4 are indexed in accordance to the JCPDS card no: 24–0734, associated with tetragonal phase group. The crystallite size calculated using Scherer formula from the dominant peak (211) at 36.4° was around 25–30 nm (Fig. 2f).

The FT-IR analysis shows the sharp intensive peak at 691 cm−1 and 726 cm−1 which represents C–C and C–H bonds respectively. C–H bending peak appears to be small at 895 cm−1, which indicates the transparent property of C-Qds. Two shoulder peaks appear at 1029 cm−1 and 1080 cm−1 representing the alkoxy C–O bond. Bands at 1378 cm−1, 1458 cm−1, and 1492 cm−1 show the Sp3 C–H bending vibrations. CO stretch has appeared at 1606 cm−1 in C-Qds. FT-IR spectrum reveal that the C-Qds contain carbon and oxygen bond S1 [27].

UV–Visible spectrum of the C-Qds shows three peaks at 207 nm, 280 nm shows the transition p-p* and 36 nm represent the n-p* S2. In the optical illumination, a pale green colour was emitted explicitly at 365 nm. Photoluminescence and photoluminescence excitation property was recorded for C-Qds in dispersion state and the maximum emission wavelength was recorded at 515 and 365 nm respectively. This specific PL property of the C-Qds may be attributed to the multiphoton active progression S3. The SAED pattern depicts the CQDs as a bright circular ring, indicating that the synthesized CQDs are crystalline S4. The presence of C–O and CO groups is responsible for the contributions at 530.69 eV and 531.9 eV, respectively S5. Mn3O4 was analysed with FT-IR, where 412 cm−1 indicates Mn3+ vibration manganese species (Mn3+) in the octahedral site of Mn3O4, and 530-550 cm−1, 639- 650 cm−1 shows the Mn–O octahedral and tetrahedral sites respectively and 1600-1640 cm−1 accounts for bending vibration group bond with Mn atom S6. SEM Image of prepared Mn3O4 shows nanospheres structured nano particle size around 100–150 nm S7.

Cyclic voltammogram for electrodes containing Mn3O4, C-Qds, C-Qds-Mn at scan rate of 20, 40, 80, 120, 160, and 200 mV/s in 6 M KOH between 0.8V and 1.6V was analysed as shown in (Fig. 3 a–d). Capacitive currents were observed for all the electrodes. Differences in ΔJ current density variation (Ja-Jc) at over potential 1.2 V was plotted against scan rate with linear regression fit to estimate the double layer capacitance Cdl. The Cdl obtained from the graph was used to calculate the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) using the formula
ECSA = Cdl /Cs ----------------------- (i)
Here Cs was assumed to be same for all electrodes (40 μFcm-2)17. The variation trend of ECSA is in line with Cdl as follows,
ECSA C-Qds-Mn (9.95) >ECSA C-Qds (6.65) >ECSA Mn3O4 (2.15) -------------- (ii)

Eqn (ii) shows the increased synergistic effect of composite C-Qds-Mn with increasing ECSA exponentially. The increase in ECSA could be attributed to the increase in conductivity, active surface area and specific activity [28]. The Turnover frequency was calculated using formula mentioned in supplementary. Based on the calculated values mentioned in the s9, the ratio CQD-Mn shows the highest value of ToF than other variants. The particles get agglomerated in catalyst Mn3O4 resulting in lower ECSA than other catalysts. Additionally the normalized LSV curves to ECSA showed that CQD-Mn has lowest potential when compared to other two variants in both HER and OER [29]. The HER and OER reactions were repeated six times at the same conditions. The mean and standard deviation values are obtained from the 6 measurements and percent error is calculated as the standard deviation divided by the average voltage. Based on the values obtained from repeated experiments under same condition, the average HER and OER activity is 0.494 and 1.6671 V respectively. The standard deviation for HER and OER is 0.006 V and 0.009 V which is comparably lower conforming the statistical significant of the compound [30].

We employed LSV to evaluate the HER and OER activities of the fabricated electrodes in 6 M KOH. For assessment, commercial ruthenium oxide was tested as well. Fig. 4 a shows the curves for HER obtained from LSV for the above stated samples at scan rate of 0.005 mV/s. This evaluation is based on the potential needed for electrode to reach 10 mAcm−1. The over potential for C-Qds-Mn to reach 10 mAcm−1 was 0.494 V vs RHE which was higher than C-Qds (0.673 V vs RHE) and Mn3O4 (1.132 V vs RHE) and comparable to RuO2 (0.465 V vs RHE). The low over potential of electrodes C-Qds and Mn3O4 proves that growth of C-Qds on manganese oxide nanoparticles plays vital role in elevating kinetic reactions of C-Qds-Mn. The different ratios were shown increased HER activity in LSV based on their CQD content. This corresponds to the increasing order of ECSA proving CQD increases the surface area which is evident for improved HER activity (Fig. 4b) shows the tafel slopes of the electrodes which reveals the C-Qds-Mn (53 mV/dec) higher than RuO2 (44 mV/dec) however much lower than other electrodes C-Qds (119 mV/dec) and Mn3O4 (203 mV/dec). This proves the favourable electrocatalytic activity of electrode C-Qds-Mn towards HER. The stability tests (Fig. 4c) show the unaltered activity of C-Qds-Mn after 1500 cycles.

Fig. 4d shows the OER activity, electrode with C-Qds-Mn requires 1.6671 V vs RHE to reach a current density of 10 mA cm−1 which is marginally higher than commercial RuO2 (1.4419 V vs RHE) and much lower than C-Qds (1.9554 V vs RHE) and Mn3O4 (2.1308 V vs RHE). This shows the greater catalytic behaviour of electrode with synergistically composed C-Qds-Mn for enhanced oxygen evolution reaction. . Just alike HER, OER also follows the increased CQD in the ratios tried (Fig. 4e) shows the tafel slopes of the electrodes with curve C-Qds-Mn (40 mV/dec), which is much smaller than C-Qds (59 mV/dec) and Mn3O4 (72 mV/dec). This suggests that the kinetic ability towards OER of C-Qds-Mn must be excellent indicating the fastest kinetic reaction and OER kinetics of the catalyst C-Qds-Mn. Further, this highest OER performance of C-Qds-Mn could be the result of high ECSA of the composite. Further the stability of prepared C-Qds-Mn was estimated in O2 saturated 6 M potassium hydroxide by cyclic voltammogram for 1500 cycles. The resultant LSV curve shows the negligible changes after 1500 cycles indicating higher stability of the electrode (Fig. 4f) Mn3O4 increases the surface area but has low conductivity which can be increased exponentially by increasing the concentration C-Qds in the composite mixture.

In summary, a simple, cost-effective, novel method was implemented to synthesize C-Qds from waste compost. Further, the microwave method was used to synthesize Mn3O4 nanospheres. Synthesized C-Qds and Mn3O4 were coated on carbon paper. The incorporation of C-Qds on manganese oxide in carbon paper increases both conductivity and kinetic reaction. The increase in the electrochemical surface area generated more active sites on the surface for the HER/OER. The agglomeration of manganese oxide greatly contributed to the stability of the electrocatalyst. ToF (Turn over Frequency) calculation used to make effective combination. Due to their increased ECSA, conductivity, and electron-hole transfer, the prepared C-Qds-Mn delivered excellent HER/OER (0.494 V/1.6671V) activity, lower Standard deviation 0.006 V and 0.009V, surpassing the 10 mA limit comparable to commercially available benchmark catalysts.
Acknowledgement
The author Dr K. Saminathan gratefully acknowledges and thank the I-STEM/Catalytic grant/acad_09/2021-22 for the financial support.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
